title: Policy conflicts & the Phillips curve
summary:
## The big idea AS Unit 5 listed the conflicts between macro objectives; A2 gives the flagship conflict — **unemployment vs inflation** — its own famous model. The **Phillips curve** began as an empirical discovery, hardened into a policy menu, collapsed spectacularly in the 1970s, and was rebuilt around **expectations**. Its story *is* the story of modern macroeconomics — and its conclusion (no long-run trade-off; only supply-side improvement escapes the conflicts) is the single most useful sentence in A2 policy essays. ## The conflicts, quickly restated - **Unemployment vs inflation** — stimulate AD to create jobs and you bid up wages and prices (this chapter's star). - **Growth vs inflation** — demand-led growth near capacity is inflationary. - **Growth vs the current account** — richer consumers import more. - **Growth vs environment/sustainability**; **efficiency/growth vs equity** — supply-side reforms can widen inequality. - **Short run vs long run** — squeezing inflation costs jobs *now* for stability *later*; supply-side reform pays off later but not now. ## The short-run Phillips curve In 1958 A.W. Phillips plotted a century of UK data and found a stable **inverse relationship between unemployment and (wage) inflation**: tight labour markets → workers scarce → wages bid up → price inflation. Policymakers read it as a **menu**: pick your preferred blend — a little more inflation buys a little less unemployment.
**What each part means:** each yellow **SRPC** slopes down — the short-run trade-off: along a given curve, lower unemployment comes with higher inflation. But each curve is drawn for a given level of **expected inflation**. Stimulate demand to push unemployment below the **NRU** and inflation rises; workers then *expect* that inflation and build it into wage demands — the whole curve **shifts up** to the dashed **SRPC2**, with the old unemployment rate back but higher inflation. Repeat, and inflation ratchets ever upward. Connecting the points where expectations have caught up gives the vertical red **LRPC** at the **natural rate of unemployment**: in the long run there is **no trade-off** to exploit.
## The breakdown: stagflation Through the 1960s the menu seemed to work. Then the 1970s delivered **stagflation** — high inflation *and* high unemployment simultaneously (oil shocks pushing SRAS left, plus expectations unanchored by years of stimulus). Points far above-right of the old curve made the simple trade-off untenable. **Friedman and Phelps had predicted exactly this**: the curve isn't a menu, because people *learn*. ## The expectations-augmented (long-run) Phillips curve Their logic: unemployment can only be held below its **natural rate (NRU / NAIRU** — the rate consistent with stable inflation, set by *supply-side* features: skills mismatch, mobility, benefits systems, union structures**)** by fooling workers with inflation they didn't expect. Once they catch on, real wages and unemployment return to where they were — at a permanently **higher inflation rate**. Hold unemployment below the NRU forever and you get **accelerating** inflation. Hence: - **The LRPC is vertical at the NRU** — the mirror of the vertical classical LRAS. - Demand policy chooses the *inflation* rate in the long run, not the *unemployment* rate. - **Only supply-side policies that lower the NRU itself** — training that fixes mismatch, mobility, sharper job-search incentives — cut unemployment without inflation. ## Living with the conflicts - **Assign tools to targets:** monetary policy → inflation; supply-side policy → the NRU and capacity; fiscal policy → stabilisation and distribution. - **Anchor expectations:** independent, inflation-targeting central banks make low inflation credible, so the SRPC stays put and disinflation costs fewer jobs — the great policy lesson of the 1990s–2000s. - **Prioritise by context:** in a deep slump (unemployment far above NRU, inflation low) stimulus faces little trade-off; near capacity the conflicts bite hard. The 2021–23 global inflation revived every one of these debates — including painful disinflation via higher interest rates. - **Supply-side improvement remains the exit door** from every conflict: it raises growth, lowers the NRU, eases inflation *and* helps the trade balance simultaneously — slowly. ## Why it matters The Phillips-curve story integrates AD/AS, expectations, the natural rate and the classical–Keynesian debate into one narrative — exactly the synthesis Paper 4 essays reward. Learn the diagram with **shifting SRPCs**, and make 'no long-run trade-off; lower the NRU' your closing argument.
## ⚡ Quick revision - Conflicts checklist: unemployment↔inflation, growth↔inflation, growth↔current account, growth↔environment, efficiency↔equity, short↔long run. - **SRPC**: inverse unemployment–inflation link, drawn for given expected inflation. - Push unemployment below **NRU** → inflation ↑ → expectations adjust → **SRPC shifts up** → accelerating inflation. - **LRPC vertical at NRU/NAIRU** → no long-run trade-off; demand policy sets only inflation long-run. - **Stagflation** (1970s) broke the simple curve; Friedman–Phelps expectations explained it. - Escape routes: **supply-side policies that lower the NRU**; independent central banks anchoring expectations.

keyTerms.definition: When achieving one macro objective worsens another.
The inverse short-run relationship between unemployment and inflation.
A vertical line at the natural rate of unemployment, implying no long-run trade-off.
The unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation, determined by supply-side factors.
The simultaneous occurrence of high inflation and high unemployment.
A Phillips curve that shifts with workers' inflation expectations.

diagrams: **Short-run Phillips curve:** downward-sloping, inflation on the y-axis and unemployment on the x-axis.

**Long-run Phillips curve:** a vertical LRPC at the natural rate, with a family of SRPCs shifting up as expected inflation rises; show the move from A to B to C when unemployment is pushed below the NRU.
Pair with **AD/AS** to link the Phillips curve to demand-pull inflation.
tips: Have the **main conflicts** ready as an evaluation checklist (unemployment–inflation, growth–current account, growth–environment, efficiency–equity).
Explain the **SRPC vs LRPC** and why the long-run curve is **vertical at the NRU** (expectations adjust).
Use **stagflation** to explain why the original Phillips curve broke down.
Conclude that **only supply-side policy that lowers the NRU** cuts unemployment without inflation.
Mention **inflation targeting / independent central banks** as ways to soften the trade-off.

revisionNotes:
# Policy conflicts & the Phillips curve — quick revision

**Conflicts checklist (evaluation skeleton)**
- Unemployment ↔ inflation (Phillips). Growth ↔ inflation. Growth ↔ current account. Growth ↔ environment. Efficiency ↔ equity. Short run ↔ long run.

**Phillips curve story (four acts)**
1. **SRPC** (Phillips 1958): inverse unemployment–inflation relation → policy 'menu'.
2. **Breakdown**: 1970s **stagflation** (oil shocks + unanchored expectations) — points off the curve.
3. **Friedman–Phelps**: each SRPC assumes given expected inflation; below-NRU unemployment → inflation → expectations adjust → **SRPC shifts up** → accelerating inflation.
4. **LRPC vertical at the NRU/NAIRU** — no long-run trade-off (mirror of classical vertical LRAS).

**NRU/NAIRU**
- Unemployment rate at stable inflation; set by **supply-side** factors (mismatch, mobility, benefits, unions).
- ⇒ only supply-side reform lowers it without inflation.

**Managing conflicts**
- Assign tools: monetary → inflation; supply-side → NRU/capacity; fiscal → stabilisation/distribution.
- **Independent inflation-targeting central banks** anchor expectations → flatter, stabler SRPC, cheaper disinflation.
- Context decides: big output gap → weak trade-off (stimulate); near capacity → sharp trade-off.

**Exam habit** — draw LRPC + at least two SRPCs with the A→B→C loop; close with 'no long-run trade-off — lower the NRU'.
questions:
# Practice questions — Policy conflicts & the Phillips curve **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 3 style — multiple choice **1.** The short-run Phillips curve shows: - A — a positive relationship between growth and inflation - B — an inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation - C — a vertical line at the natural rate of unemployment - D — the trade-off between growth and the current account
▸ Show answer

B. Lower unemployment ↔ higher inflation along a given short-run curve — the original Phillips finding. C describes the long-run curve.

**2.** According to the expectations-augmented Phillips curve, holding unemployment below the natural rate permanently results in: - A — a constant, higher rate of inflation - B — accelerating inflation - C — deflation - D — a lower natural rate
▸ Show answer

B. Each period workers build the latest inflation into expectations, shifting the SRPC up — so keeping unemployment 'too low' requires ever-faster inflation.

**3.** Stagflation in the 1970s was significant because it: - A — confirmed the original Phillips curve - B — combined high inflation with high unemployment, contradicting the simple trade-off - C — eliminated unemployment in most economies - D — proved that supply-side policy causes inflation
▸ Show answer

B. The simple curve said the two evils moved in opposite directions; oil shocks and unanchored expectations delivered both at once, forcing the expectations rebuild.

**4.** Which policy would reduce the natural rate of unemployment? - A — an increase in aggregate demand - B — a cut in interest rates - C — retraining programmes that reduce the mismatch between workers' skills and vacancies - D — a rise in unemployment benefits
▸ Show answer

C. The NRU is a supply-side phenomenon (mismatch, mobility, incentives). Demand policies (A, B) move the economy along/around the curves; D would tend to raise the NRU.

## Paper 4 style — data response > An economy enjoys unemployment of 3.5% — well below the 5.5% most economists estimate as its natural rate — after two years of aggressive fiscal and monetary stimulus. Inflation has climbed from 2% to 6% and wage settlements are accelerating as unions demand 'compensation for the cost of living'. The central bank, newly granted independence, announces a 2% inflation target and raises interest rates sharply. The finance minister protests that 'this will throw people out of work merely to hit a number'. **(a)** Define the 'natural rate of unemployment'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why wage settlements are accelerating. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse, using short-run and long-run Phillips curves, the economy's position and the likely effect of the central bank's tightening. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the finance minister is right that the disinflation is not worth the unemployment cost. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The unemployment rate consistent with stable inflation (NAIRU), determined by supply-side features of the labour market. [1+1]

(b) Unemployment below the NRU → labour scarce → bargaining power with workers; and inflation expectations have risen (6% built into 'cost of living' claims) — the wage–price spiral mechanism shifting the SRPC up. [4]

(c) Economy at a point below-left of NRU on an SRPC that has already shifted up (2% → 6%). Tightening moves the economy back along/up: unemployment rises toward 5.5% while inflation falls; once expectations re-anchor at 2%, the SRPC shifts back down and the economy returns to the NRU with low inflation — the A→B→C loop reversed. Diagram expected. [6]

(d) Minister's point: real short-run job losses, hardship. Bank's case: the 3.5% was never sustainable — the choice is disinflation now or accelerating inflation and a harsher squeeze later; early action by a credible independent bank re-anchors expectations at lower unemployment cost (a smaller 'sacrifice ratio'). Judgement: tightening defensible; complement with supply-side measures to lower the NRU itself. [8]

## Paper 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain why the original Phillips curve appeared to offer governments a policy choice, and why Friedman and Phelps argued the choice was an illusion. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether there is any trade-off between unemployment and inflation that policy can exploit. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) SRPC as menu (trade some inflation for jobs); expectations adjustment → SRPC shifts → vertical LRPC at NRU → only accelerating inflation from exploiting it.

(b) Short run: yes — especially with slack and anchored expectations (flat modern Phillips curves let demand run further). Long run: no — NRU rules. Nuances: hysteresis means long slumps raise the NRU, so preventing unemployment persistence has supply-side value; credibility changes the terms. Judgement: temporary, state-dependent trade-off; no permanent one.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain **three** conflicts between macroeconomic objectives, other than that between unemployment and inflation. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss the view that supply-side policy is the only way a government can escape the conflicts between its macroeconomic objectives. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Growth vs inflation; growth vs current account (imports); growth vs environment; efficiency vs equity; short vs long run — any three with mechanisms.

(b) For: LRAS/NRU improvements advance growth, jobs, price stability and competitiveness together — dissolving rather than trading off. Against 'only': slow and uncertain — demand management still needed for shocks; credibility devices (independent central banks) also soften trade-offs; some conflicts (growth vs environment) need pricing externalities, not just capacity. Judgement: supply-side is the long-run escape; policy assignment and credibility manage the meantime.
